exemplar.title: How does varying the temperature of the air inside a tube affect the velocity of sound travelling inside the tube?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does varying the temperature of the air inside a tube affect the velocity of sound travelling inside the tube?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear presentation of raw and processed data with units and uncertainties
Identification and justified exclusion of the anomalous data point
Appropriate and mostly accurate processing (averaging, linear regressions)
weaknesses: Inconsistent significant-figure usage and mis-labelled graph axis
Incomplete propagation of all uncertainties (tuning fork, temperature)
Omissions in justifying final uncertainty rounding and limited precision in uncertainty treatment
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly or precisely, and calculations are largely accurate, fitting the 3-4 band. Consideration of uncertainties shows significant omissions, placing it at the lower end of the band. A score of 4 appropriately balances strengths against these gaps.

strengths: Well-defined, discipline-specific context linking tube resonance and sound-speed theory with clear equations and diagrams
Detailed, numbered method with annotated apparatus list and uncertainty quotations enabling reproducibility
Logical derivation of key equations and clear identification of variables with units
weaknesses: Minor ambiguities in procedure (e.g., tube movement speed, judgement of âlouderâ)
End-correction term defined but not quantified or discussed
Spelling error in heading and omission of tube material properties
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets the 5-6 band for research question context and methodological reproducibility, demonstrating a focused question in a specific context and a largely complete procedure. Methodological considerations are described but not fully explained (3-4 band), so a holistic score of 5 reflects this high performance with minor omissions.

strengths: Conclusion correctly restates the linear trend and links to hypotheses with reference to RÂ²
Relevant comparison of gradient and intercept to accepted scientific context with percentage errors
weaknesses: Overstated confidence given data scatter and RÂ² value
Limited breadth in scientific context comparison (only one temperature point)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is consistent with the analysis and includes relevant scientific comparison (3-4 band), but confidence claims are overstated and context discussion is brief. A score of 4 reflects solid performance with room for deeper justification.

strengths: Identification of specific methodological weaknesses with clear links to their effects on results
Realistic, relevant improvements explained in terms of how they would reduce systematic and random errors
weaknesses: Depth of explanation varies across suggested improvements
No quantification of the anticipated impact of each improvement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation explains the relative impact of specific weaknesses and proposes improvements with clear mechanistic explanations, satisfying most descriptors of the highest band. Minor variability in depth justifies a score of 5 rather than 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states a focused question and embeds it in a well-defined, discipline-specific context.  The introduction links temperature, tube resonance and the theoretical speed-of-sound relationship through relevant equations (Eq. 1â6) and diagrams.  This establishes exactly why a tube of air is an appropriate system and why temperature is the salient variable, satisfying the descriptor for a specific and appropriate context.

Instrument uncertainties are recorded, propagated for LT and gradients, and represented by error bars.  Fork uncertainty is dismissed without justification and temperature uncertainty is not propagated into v.  Treating every Îv as a fixed Â±20 m sâ»Â¹ irrespective of slope breadth reveals omissions.  Consideration is present but with significant gaps, placing the work at the lower end of the 3-4 band.

Independent, dependent and controlled variables are identified and briefly justified; the influence of tube material, air density and diameter is described.  However the student does not fully explain why the chosen frequency range or number of repeats is sufficient, nor how end correction is treated.  Thus methodological considerations are described rather than fully explained, aligning with the 3-4 band.

Four specific weaknesses (temperature drift, manual resonance detection, variable fork-tube distance, ambient noise) are identified and linked to over- or underestimation of v and to anomalous points, thereby explaining relative impact.  The discussion is clear though depth is variable; nonetheless it exceeds mere description, fitting the 5-6 band at 5.

A numbered method, complete apparatus list and annotated set-up diagram allow almost full replication.  Temperatures, frequencies, measurement devices and their uncertainties are quoted; step-by-step instructions describe how each reading is taken three times at five temperatures.  Minor ambiguities (e.g. precise tube movement speed, how âlouderâ is judged) keep the work from perfection but overall the procedure is reproduceable, matching the top-band description.

Averaging, calculation of 1â4f, linear regressions and gradient extraction are carried out consistently for each temperature.  An anomalous data set is identified and rationally excluded.  Minor flaws exist (gradient quoted as v yet intercept retained; rounding errors) but there are no major inaccuracies.  Processing is therefore appropriate and mostly accurate, meriting 4.

For each weakness a concrete, feasible improvement is proposed (e.g., digital thermometer with data logger, sound-analysis software, clamped fork with laser distance meter, acoustic damping) and the mechanism by which it would reduce error is explained.  This satisfies the âexplainedâ criterion for the highest band; a score of 5 is warranted.

Raw and processed tables are generally clear, include units and uncertainties and sample calculations are shown.  Graphs have labelled axes and error bars, though some tables (20 Â°C) suffer formatting issues and the y-axis of Graph 2 is mis-labelled with âK.â  Overall communication is clear if not flawless, satisfying âclear OR preciseâ; a mark of 4 is appropriate.

The student compares both gradient and intercept to the accepted relation v = 0.60 T + 331 and to the standard value at 20 Â°C, calculating percentage errors and commenting on agreement.  This is a relevant and reasoned comparison, though justification is brief and limited to two references, so the strand fits the 3-4 descriptor.

The conclusion restates the observed linear increase of v with T and links it to the hypothesis, referencing the RÂ² value and uncertainty analysis.  It discusses the impact of the excluded 174 Hz data.  While largely consistent with the analysis, confidence is somewhat overstated given the scatter, hence âdescribed but not fully consistentââtop of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively situates the research question within the discipline, linking tube resonance and sound speed theory. The inclusion of equations and diagrams establishes clear context for the experiment.
The heading âRESEARCH OUESTIONâ contains a spelling error. Correcting this and ensuring consistent formatting would improve clarity and professionalism.
The scientific background section clearly explains standingâwave formation and harmonic conditions, referencing reputable sources. This demonstrates a solid grasp of underlying principles.
Equation [3] is derived logically by combining prior relations, clearly showing how plotting $L_T$ vs $\frac{1}{4f}$ yields the sound velocity as the gradient.
The endâcorrection term $e$ is defined but later not quantified or discussed in data processing. The student should explain how $e$ was determined or justify its omission.
Although Equation [6] offers a linear approximation for $v(T)$, the student should justify why the curvature in Equation [5] can be neglected over the chosen temperature range.
The hypothesis alternates between $v\propto T$ and $v\propto\sqrt T$, creating inconsistency. The student should clarify the proportionality based on the derived equations.
Variables are well identified with units and measurement methods, facilitating reproducibility. The structured tables support clarity in variable control.
The ethical considerations section simply states âno ethical considerations.â Even for a physics experiment, a brief note on data integrity or safety protocols would demonstrate thoroughness.
Apparatus list includes relevant items but omits the tubeâs exact material properties (e.g., wall thickness). Specifying these would aid reproducibility.
Step 7 in the method lacks a clear criterion for stabilizing temperature at 71 Â°C. The student should specify how long to wait and how equilibrium is verified.
Instruction to âloosen the clampâ is vague. The student should quantify the allowed movement or provide a diagram illustrating the correct clamp tension.
Processing of $L_T$ is clearly shown with correct propagation of length uncertainties, demonstrating precise communication of calculations.
The table of $L_T$ values shows inconsistent rounding (e.g., 0.083 vs. 0.087). Applying uniform significantâfigure rules would enhance precision.
Discarding tuning fork uncertainty overlooks a nonzero but small source. Including it in propagation would demonstrate thoroughness, even if it contributes minimally.
Identification and exclusion of the anomalous 174 Hz point is well justified, improving data modeling by focusing on the valid trend.
Uncertainty calculation for $v$ uses only gradient extremes. The student should also consider random error contributions from $L_T$ uncertainties.
The calculation of $\Delta v = (400-368)/2$ is correct, but representing the final uncertainty as Â±16 msâ»Â¹ to 1 s.f. before rounding to Â±20 msâ»Â¹ needs clearer justification.
Graph 2âs yâaxis label appears as âK.â instead of velocity units. Correct labelling is essential for precise data communication.
The uncertainty and accuracy errorâanalysis is comprehensive, comparing both gradient and intercept to literature values, demonstrating clear understanding of dataâanalysis techniques.
The conclusion restates the positive linear trend and links to the hypothesis well; referencing $R^2$ demonstrates consistency with analysis.
Confidence in results is overstated given an $R^2$ of 0.8466 and high gradient uncertainty. The student should moderate claims and discuss scatter effects.
Comparison to literature is limited to one temperature point. For deeper scientific context, include comparison across a broader temperature range if data exist.
The evaluation table identifies specific systematic and random errors and links each to its effect, explaining their relative impact on results.
Proposed improvements are feasible and clearly explained regarding how they would reduce specific errors, fulfilling the highest evaluation band.
To further strengthen the evaluation, the student could quantify the anticipated improvement from each suggested change, illustrating relative benefit.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
